Jules Kourelakos
home blog garden

Nash Equilibrium

Created March 15, 2026 · Last updated March 15, 2026

Dominance & Equilibrium

Core concepts for analyzing strategic games: how players reason about optimal choices and when outcomes “settle.”

Dominant Strategy

A dominant strategy is the best strategy to play regardless of what strategy the other player plays.

  • If a game has a dominant strategy for all players, rational players will always follow it.
  • A dominant strategy produces the best (or tied-best) outcome no matter what the opponent does.
  • Not all games have a dominant strategy — see Classic Games.

Weak vs. Strong Domination

TypeDefinition
Strong dominationPayoff is always strictly better than every other strategy
Weak dominationPayoff is always better or equal to every other strategy

Nash Equilibrium (NE)

A Nash Equilibrium is the best strategy to play given the strategy the other player is actually using.

  • Weaker than a dominant strategy — it only holds conditional on the opponent’s choice.
  • A game can have zero, one, or multiple Nash Equilibria.
  • Nash’s theorem: all finite competitive games have at least one Nash equilibrium (in pure or mixed strategies).

How to Find Nash Equilibria

  1. For each column, find the highest payoff to the column player (Player 2) in that column.
  2. Check whether the row player’s (Player 1’s) payoff in that same box is also their highest payoff across that row.
  3. If yes → that box is a NE, and the two strategies that produce it are NE strategies.
  4. If no → there is no NE in that column.
  5. Repeat for every column.

Equilibrium

An equilibrium is a settled state of affairs in which all parties have finished making their choices.

TypeDescription
Stable equilibriumCannot be easily changed by outside factors
Unstable equilibriumSusceptible to change from outside pressures

Strategy Types

TypeDescription
Pure strategyAlways makes the same response to a given move
Mixed strategyMay respond differently to the same move depending on circumstances

Game Representations

FormDescription
Extensive formTree diagram — shows the sequence of moves
Strategic formBox (matrix) diagram — shows all strategy combinations simultaneously

Focal Points

A focal point (Schelling point) is a Nash Equilibrium that has some conspicuous, prominent, or salient feature that each player can reasonably expect the other to notice — increasing both players’ propensity to choose it even without communication.

Example: In the Stag Hunt, mutual cooperation (Hunt Stag / Hunt Stag) is the focal point because it is both better for everyone and uniquely prominent.

Focal-point reasoning works best when the preferred NE is clearly superior. It is less effective when switching toward the better equilibrium risks leaving one player worse off if the other doesn’t follow.
